reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Wednesday in mid-May. Nothing about today is dramatic. The week isn't over, the weekend isn't here, the season hasn't quite turned. This is the kind of day I used to drink through without noticing — a Wednesday I would have erased without a second thought. Now it's the day I actually live in.

When I was 22 and white-knuckling the idea of getting sober, I thought the hard part would be the parties, the holidays, the breakups. Turns out those have their own gravity — they wake you up. The real test is Wednesday. A normal afternoon with no crisis to manage and no celebration to ride. The texture of an ordinary day is where a life is actually built or quietly lost. Durant's line about excellence being a habit isn't about ambition — it's about who you are when nobody's watching, when nothing is happening.

Eleven years in, I've come to believe sobriety lives or dies on the ordinary Wednesday. Did I sleep? Did I move my body? Did I call someone back? Did I sit with a feeling for ten minutes instead of escaping it? Those aren't dramatic acts. They're the small, repeated motions that compound into a person you don't need to run from. The smaller life I was afraid of turned out to be the smaller drunk life. This one is bigger, and most of it is Wednesdays.
reflection.meditation: What is one ordinary thing I can do today, not because it's exciting, but because it's mine to do?

affirmations: I am building a life one ordinary day at a time, and today counts.
Today I can sit with discomfort instead of running from it.
I am someone who shows up — for myself, for the people in my life, for the small things.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Wednesday with nothing on fire. Grateful for the people who picked up when I called, the coffee that tasted like coffee, the body that woke up clear. Grateful that I get to find out what an ordinary day actually feels like — something I didn't know was possible for most of my twenties.
gratitude.closing: One more day, awake for it.
